content:
# Deuteronomy 26 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA** | EMET vs. LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 19 verses

---

## V.1 — Entry conditions

- **EMET:** "and you take possession of it and dwell in it"
- **LOGOS:** "and have taken possession of it and live in it"
- **PNEUMA:** "and you have settled there" (omits "taken possession of it")

**Differences:** PNEUMA omits yerashta ("taken possession") — collapses two clauses into one. EMET/LOGOS preserve both verbs. **Resolution:** "taken possession of it and dwell in it" — both verbs retained.

---

## V.2 — Firstfruits

- **EMET:** "from the first of all the fruit of the ground [bikkurim]"
- **LOGOS:** "some of the first of all the fruit of the ground"
- **PNEUMA:** "some of the firstfruits of all that you produce from the soil"

**Differences:** (1) EMET retains [bikkurim] gloss. (2) LOGOS/PNEUMA add "some of" (a legitimate qualification of me-reshit). (3) PNEUMA: "all that you produce" for "fruit of the ground" — more functional. **Resolution:** "from the first of all the fruit of the ground [bikkurim]" — EMET; the [bikkurim] gloss aids the reader.

---

## V.2 — "Make his name dwell"

- **EMET:** "to cause his name to dwell there"
- **LOGOS:** "to make his name to dwell there"
- **PNEUMA:** "as a dwelling for his name"

**Differences:** Minor wording variation on leshakem shmo sham. All three convey the same meaning. **Resolution:** "to make his name to dwell there" — LOGOS.

---

## V.3 — "In those days" vs. "at that time"

- **EMET:** "the priest who is [in office] in those days"
- **LOGOS:** "the priest who is in office at that time"
- **PNEUMA:** "the priest who is serving at that time"

**Differences:** "In those days" (ba-yamim ha-hem) vs. "at that time." EMET preserves the temporal phrase more literally. PNEUMA adds "serving" — not in the Hebrew. **Resolution:** "the priest who is in office in those days" — EMET.

---

## V.3 — "Our fathers" vs. "our ancestors"

- **EMET/LOGOS:** "our fathers"
- **PNEUMA:** "our ancestors"

**Differences:** PNEUMA consistently renders avotenu as "ancestors" (gender-neutral). Project convention uses "fathers." **Resolution:** "our fathers" — consistent with project conventions.

---

## V.5 — Opening formula for the creed

- **EMET:** "And you shall answer and say before the LORD your God"
- **LOGOS:** "And you shall make response before the LORD your God"
- **PNEUMA:** "Then you shall declare in the presence of the LORD your God"

**Differences:** (1) LOGOS: "make response" — captures the liturgical, responsive character of anita ve-amarta. (2) PNEUMA: "declare" — flattens the dialogical quality. (3) EMET: "answer and say" — most literal. **Resolution:** "And you shall make response before the LORD your God" — LOGOS's rendering of the formal liturgical formula.

---

## V.5 — "Few people" vs. "few in number"

- **EMET:** "with a few people"
- **LOGOS:** "few in number"
- **PNEUMA:** "with only a few people and lived there as a foreigner"

**Differences:** PNEUMA adds "lived there as a foreigner" — anticipating v.5b "sojourned." EMET/LOGOS are more concise. **Resolution:** "with a few people" — EMET.

---

## V.6 — Egypt's treatment

- **EMET:** "dealt harshly with us, and afflicted us, and put upon us hard service"
- **LOGOS:** "treated us harshly and humiliated us and laid on us hard labor"
- **PNEUMA:** "treated us harshly and oppressed us, forcing us into brutal labor"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "afflicted us" (anenu); LOGOS: "humiliated us"; PNEUMA: "oppressed us." The Hebrew root anah means to afflict/oppress/humble. (2) PNEUMA: "brutal labor" for avodah qashah ("hard service") — interpretive. (3) LOGOS: "humiliated" is an unusual choice for anah. **Resolution:** "treated us harshly and afflicted us and laid on us hard labor" — blending EMET and LOGOS.

---

## V.7 — "Hardship" terms

- **EMET:** "our affliction and our toil and our oppression"
- **LOGOS:** "our affliction, our toil, and our oppression"
- **PNEUMA:** "our misery, our hardship, and our distress"

**Differences:** PNEUMA entirely reworks the three Hebrew terms: oni (affliction), amal (toil/labor), lachats (oppression/pressing). "Toil" and "affliction" are more literal. PNEUMA's "hardship" and "distress" are functional but less precise. **Resolution:** "our affliction, our toil, and our oppression" — LOGOS.

---

## V.8 — "Mighty hand" phrasing

- **EMET:** "with a strong hand and with an outstretched arm, and with great terror, and with signs and with wonders"
- **LOGOS:** "with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great deeds of terror, with signs and wonders"
- **PNEUMA:** "with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with terrifying power, with miraculous signs and wonders"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "strong hand" vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA: "mighty hand" for yad chazaqah. Both are valid; "mighty hand" is the standard formula. (2) EMET: "great terror" (mora gadol); LOGOS: "great deeds of terror"; PNEUMA: "terrifying power." The Hebrew mora means fear/terror, not "deeds." **Resolution:** "with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great terror, with signs and wonders."

---

## V.10 — Tense and deictic particle

- **EMET:** "And now, behold, I have brought the first of the fruit of the ground"
- **LOGOS:** "And behold, now I bring the first of the fruit of the ground"
- **PNEUMA:** "And now I am bringing the firstfruits of the soil"

**Differences:** The Hebrew we-attah hineh heveiti uses hineh (behold) with a perfect verb. EMET: "behold, I have brought"; LOGOS: "behold, now I bring"; PNEUMA omits hineh. **Resolution:** "And now, behold, I bring the first of the fruit of the ground" — retains hineh, present-tense rendering.

---

## V.10 — Direct address shift

- **EMET:** "that you have given me, O LORD" — preserves direct address
- **LOGOS:** "which you, O LORD, have given me" — preserves direct address
- **PNEUMA:** "that you, LORD, have given me" — slightly less formal

**Differences:** Minor: "O LORD" vs. "LORD." **Resolution:** "that you, O LORD, have given me" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

## V.11 — "Sojourner" vs. "foreigner" / "the Levite"

- **EMET:** "you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is in your midst"
- **LOGOS:** "you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is among you"
- **PNEUMA:** "you and the Levites and the foreigners among you"

**Differences:** (1) PNEUMA: "the Levites" (plural); EMET/LOGOS: "the Levite" (singular, ha-Levi). (2) PNEUMA: "foreigners" for ger; EMET/LOGOS: "sojourner." Project convention is "sojourner" for ger. **Resolution:** "you, and the Levite, and the sojourner who is in your midst" — EMET.

---

## V.12 — "Paying" vs. "tithing" vs. "setting aside"

- **EMET:** "when you have finished tithing all the tithe of your produce"
- **LOGOS:** "when you have finished paying all the tithe of your produce"
- **PNEUMA:** "when you have finished setting aside a tenth of all your produce"

**Differences:** EMET/LOGOS translate the Piel of ashar differently: "tithing" vs. "paying." PNEUMA: "setting aside a tenth" — functional paraphrase. **Resolution:** "when you have finished paying all the tithe of your produce" — LOGOS.

---

## V.13 — "Transgressed" vs. "deviated"

- **EMET:** "I have not transgressed your commandments, nor have I forgotten"
- **LOGOS:** "I have not transgressed any of your commandments, nor have I forgotten them"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have not deviated from your commands or forgotten any of them"

**Differences:** "Transgressed" (avar) vs. PNEUMA "deviated." EMET/LOGOS: "transgressed" is the standard cultic term. **Resolution:** "I have not transgressed any of your commandments, nor have I forgotten them" — LOGOS.

---

## V.14 — "In my mourning" — Hebrew be-oni

- **EMET:** "I have not eaten of it in my mourning"
- **LOGOS:** "I have not eaten of the tithe while I was mourning"
- **PNEUMA:** "I have not eaten any of the sacred portion while I was grieving"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "in my mourning" (be-oni); LOGOS: "while I was mourning"; PNEUMA: "while I was grieving." Minor variation. (2) LOGOS/PNEUMA add "the tithe"/"the sacred portion" for clarity. **Resolution:** "I have not eaten of it while I was mourning" — blends EMET and LOGOS.

---

## V.14 — "For the dead"

- **EMET:** "nor given any of it for the dead"
- **LOGOS:** "or offered any of it to the dead"
- **PNEUMA:** "nor have I offered any of it for the dead"

**Differences:** "Given... for the dead" (EMET) vs. "offered... to the dead" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew natatiy le-met — "given to the dead." LOGOS: "offered" implies a more ritual act than "given." **Resolution:** "nor given any of it for the dead" — EMET.

---

## V.16 — "Statutes and rules" vs. "statutes and ordinances"

- **EMET:** "these statutes and the ordinances"
- **LOGOS:** "these statutes and rules"
- **PNEUMA:** "these decrees and laws"

**Differences:** EMET: "statutes" (chuqqim) and "ordinances" (mishpatim); LOGOS: "statutes and rules"; PNEUMA: "decrees and laws." Project convention is "statutes" for chuqqim and "ordinances/rules" for mishpatim. **Resolution:** "these statutes and the ordinances" — EMET.

---

## V.16 — "Soul" vs. "being"

- **EMET:** "with all your heart and with all your being [nephesh]"
- **LOGOS:** "with all your heart and with all your soul"
- **PNEUMA:** "with all your heart and with all your soul"

**Differences:** EMET preserves [nephesh] gloss and translates as "being." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "soul." Project convention for nephesh in Deuteronomy is typically "soul" in formal contexts. **Resolution:** "with all your heart and with all your soul" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. (Note: EMET's [nephesh] gloss noted here.)

---

## V.18 — "Treasured possession" vs. "people of his own possession"

- **EMET:** "a people of his own possession [am segullah]"
- **LOGOS:** "a people for his treasured possession [am segullah]"
- **PNEUMA:** "his treasured people"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "people of his own possession" — literal. (2) LOGOS: "treasured possession" — interprets segullah. (3) PNEUMA omits the [am segullah] gloss. **Resolution:** "a people of his own possession [am segullah]" — EMET; retains the technical term with gloss.

---

## V.19 — "Renown" vs. "fame"

- **EMET:** "in praise and in renown and in splendor"
- **LOGOS:** "in praise and in fame and in honor"
- **PNEUMA:** "in praise and fame and honor"

**Differences:** EMET: "renown and splendor" for shem and tiferet; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "fame and honor." The Hebrew shem can mean "name/fame/renown." Tiferet means "glory/splendor/beauty/honor." **Resolution:** "in praise and in fame and in splendor" — shem as "fame" is more idiomatic; tiferet as "splendor" more precise than "honor."

---

*Total disagreements logged: 20*
*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
